title: Coppice hazel hurdle post
subtitle: A rived and pointed upright stake for a wattle hurdle panel
excerpt: A hurdle post is the vertical element of a wattle hurdle: a straight hazel stake, 150 cm long and 5 cm diameter, with a driven point at one end. Rived from coppice hazel, it provides the upright frame through which horizontal rods weave. This entry covers riving, pointing, and the sail or mortise cuts that let the rods pass.
sourceNotes: Traditional British hurdle-making; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for hurdle post dimensions and saucy cutting method.

content.text: A wattle hurdle; the traditional portable fence panel used for sheep hurdles and garden windbreaks; is built around its vertical posts. The posts are called saucies in the Midlands dialect: straight-grained
coppice
stakes that carry the load of the woven rods and hold the panel in shape. This entry covers making a hurdle post from
green wood
hazel: selecting, riving if needed, pointing the driven end, and cutting the openings through which the weaving rods pass.
The wood

A straight hazel rod from a coppice stool: 155 cm long, 5 to 6 cm diameter. If the rod is larger than 6 cm, rive it in half with a froe and beetle to produce two posts. The rod must be knot-free in the working length; a knot stops the weaving rod from passing cleanly through. Ash makes a stiffer, longer-lasting post; willow is too flexible for a rigid hurdle. Freshly cut winter coppice is the ideal material; summer-cut hazel is workable but the bark strips less cleanly.
Tools
A Yorkshire billhook for harvesting and trimming side branches. A froe and beetle mallet if the rod needs
riving
to the right diameter. A sloyd knife for pointing and cutting the rod-passage openings. A leather strop.
Method

If the rod is over 6 cm diameter, rive it in half: set the froe on the end grain at the centre and lever the split through the full length. Each half becomes one post. Select the half with the more consistent diameter.

De-branch the post using the billhook. Strip side branches flush with the main rod; any stub that protrudes will catch the weaving rods. Work from the base end upward, keeping the blade path parallel to the main rod.

Point the base end: four facets tapering to a blunt point over the bottom 10 cm. Use push cuts with the sloyd knife along the grain. Keep the point blunt; 15 mm across the tip; to drive cleanly without splitting in stony ground.

Cut the rod-passage openings (sometimes called the sails): a simple slot or a through-cut at each weaving level; every 20 to 25 cm up the post; to allow the horizontal hazel rods to pass through. For a simple slot, make two stop cuts 2 cm apart perpendicular to the grain, then lever out the waste. The slot should be just wide enough for a 2 cm hazel rod to pass with a slight resistance.

Allow the posts to season for 2 weeks before weaving the hurdle. Fully green posts will shrink as the hurdle dries, loosening the weave joints. Partially dried posts stabilise before the hurdle is complete.
Finishing
Leave unfinished. Hurdle posts are outdoor structural elements; any finish traps moisture at the driven end and accelerates decay. The natural tannins in hazel provide some inherent decay resistance.
Variations
Ash post: stiffer and harder than hazel, lasts longer in the ground. The same riving and pointing process applies.
Simple garden stake: omit the rod-passage slots; use as a plant support or fence upright without weaving.
Care

Drive the point fully into the ground with a mallet; do not leave the bottom 10 cm exposed above soil level. A post driven to depth sits stable and drains water away from the point. Replace posts when they show decay at the soil line; the weaving rods usually outlast the posts by several years.

attrs.intro: Common problems with coppice hazel hurdle posts.

items.fix: Keep the point blunt; 15 mm tip diameter minimum. Check for knots before beginning; a knot within 10 cm of the point will cause it to split when driven.
items.cause: Point too sharp, or knot at the base of the point zone
items.symptom: Post splits at the point when driven

items.fix: The slot should be 2 mm wider than the rod diameter to allow for seasonal movement. Enlarge tight slots with a second sloyd knife pass.
items.cause: Slots cut to the rod diameter rather than fractionally larger
items.symptom: Rod-passage slots too tight for weaving rods

items.fix: For a 120 cm high hurdle, posts should be 5 cm minimum diameter. Smaller-diameter posts work for low sheep hurdles but not for garden wind barriers where lateral load is higher.
items.cause: Post diameter too small for the hurdle height
items.symptom: Post collapses inward under hurdle load

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
